My reasoning was the following: the `stdout` and `stderr` under Pycharm are inherently bugged. These two streams are unambiguously accessible through `__stdout__` and `__stderr__`. If the user happens to override `sys.stdout`, then we do not have much information about the stream that will replace it (it could be a `file` or a `StringIO`), so we should not assume that the replacing stream is bugged too, instead we should check as usual using `stream.isatty()`.

Basically, the edge case is an user replacing `sys.stdout` with a non-tty stream on Pycharm. In such a case, `colorama.is_a_tty(sys.stdout)` should return `False`, but this would not be the case if the check is made using `stream is sys.stdout`.

Tasks with the QUEUED state should also be counted below, but for now we cannot count them. This is because there is no guarantee that queued tasks in failed dagruns will or will not eventually run and queued tasks that will never run will consume slots and can stall a DAG. Once we can guarantee that all queued tasks in failed dagruns will never run (e.g. make sure that all running/newly queued TIs have running dagruns), then we can include QUEUED tasks here, with the constraint that they are in running dagruns.

As we discussed, you can think of floating point math as creating a bell curve of possible results. If the "correct" answer is 0.5, the floating point math might result in numbers between 0.4999996 and 0.5000004. With this EPSILON, we move the threshold so that it sits on one side of the entire bell curve, so results will be consistent no matter where it landed in the bell curve.

> although Invenio is fully Open Source, it is true that many developers are salaried to primarily work on a service that happens to be based on Invenio. Therefore they are genuinely starting from a position where they contribute to this community because they have to.

Nuance: there are notably more Invenio contributors who are payed to work on various services than there are developers who are payed to work on the Invenio core itself.  Hence occasional dilemmas like "do I make this feature more general for others to directly reuse or do I stop here at my service boundaries?" which is a sort of "want to", not "have to" situation.

However, please note that the above phrase comes from the Python Code of Conduct "as is"; I have not changed anything.  I find it simpler to endorse some existing text rather than start inventing our own.

> Additionally I would add a 4th point. [...] Generous.

While I like the spirit of your point, I'm somewhat concerned that we would be modifying Python's vanilla Code of Conduct instead of citing it literally.  People may then question why this-or-this was added, removed, or modified.  A certain elegance behind my proposal, if I may say so, was that we would be simply endorsing two "well established" sets of guiding principles, which I hoped wouldn't be too controversial.  (Especially since Invenio is a Pythonic project.)  So, instead of modifying Python's upstream Code of Conduct, I'd rather prefer if you point to some other well established standard text, if you feel like adding an explicit point about generosity?
